1: wayyišʾălû bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl bĕYHWH — 'the sons of Israel inquired of YHWH.' The verb šāʾal (ask, inquire) denotes oracular consultation — seeking YHWH's directive before military action. It is the same root as the name Šāʾûl (Saul). The failure to inquire at Gibeon (Joshua 9:14) brought disaster; here its presence opens the book rightly. The question 'Who shall go up first?' reflects the need to know the vanguard role in a post-Joshua transition.
2: YHWH ʾāmar yĕhûdāh yaʿăleh hinnēh nātattî ʾet-hāʾāreṣ bĕyādô — 'YHWH said: Judah shall go up; behold, I have given the land into his hand.' The nātattî is the divine proleptic perfect — a qatal form expressing certainty of future fulfillment as though already accomplished. The verb precedes any human action; YHWH's giving renders the outcome certain before the first battle.
3: Judah invites Simeon: ûbāʾtā ʾittî bĕgôrālî — 'come up with me into my allotted portion.' The gôrāl (lot, allotted territory) is the distributive term from the Joshua allotments. Simeon's territory was embedded within Judah's (Joshua 19:1), making the partnership geographic as well as covenantal; their cooperation reflects mutual obligation within the distribution Joshua authorized.
4: wayyittēn YHWH ʾet-hakkĕnaʿănî wĕʾet-happĕrizzî bĕyādām — 'YHWH gave the Canaanites and the Perizzites into their hand.' The wayyiqtol narrative places YHWH as subject of the decisive giving (nātan bĕyād = give into the hand), the standard conquest-gift formula. Israel strikes, but YHWH gives — the syntactic subjects carry the theological weight.
5: ʾĂdōnî-bezeq — 'lord of Bezek.' The title ʾădōnî (my lord) is a throne-title rather than a personal name, marking the political claim being contested. The Canaanite/Perizzite leadership at Bezek is signaled by its lordship-title format, common in ANE city-state governance.
6: Adoni-bezek fled (wayyānos) but was caught and had thumbs and big toes cut off (bĕhōnôt yādāyw wĕraglāyw). The verb qissēṣ (pi'el of qāṣaṣ, cut off, sever) describes a standard ANE humiliation of captured enemy commanders: removing thumbs prevented sword-grip; removing big toes prevented stable combat stance. The procedure was degradation rather than execution.
7: šibʿîm mĕlākîm bĕhōnôt yĕdêhem wĕraglêhem mĕqussāṣîm — 'seventy kings with their thumbs and big toes cut off.' Adoni-bezek's self-condemnation applies the lex talionis principle: kăʾašer ʿāśîtî kēn šillem lî ʾĕlōhîm — 'as I have done, so God has repaid me.' The verb šillem (pi'el of šālam, make complete, pay in full, requite) is the same root as šālôm; justice restores the wholeness violated by the original act. Seventy is formulaic for completeness rather than a precise count.
8: wayyillaḥămû bĕnê yĕhûdāh bîrûšālaim wayyilkĕdû ʾōtāh — 'the men of Judah fought against Jerusalem and captured it.' The verb lākad (capture, seize) is the standard conquest term. The apparent contradiction with Judges 1:21 (Benjamin did not expel the Jebusites) likely reflects temporary capture and burning followed by Jebusite reoccupation — a pattern persisting until David (2 Samuel 5:6-9).
9: After Jerusalem, Judah campaigns across three geographic zones: hāhār wĕhannegeb wĕhašĕpēlāh — the hill country, the Negeb, and the Shephelah. These three terms cover the full vertical and horizontal range of Judah's allotment, signaling systematic territorial consolidation organized by terrain type rather than political boundaries.
10: wĕšēm-ḥebrôn lĕpānîm qiryat-ʾarbāʿ — the former name of Hebron was Kiriath-arba. Qiryat-ʾarbāʿ means either 'city of Arba' (the great Anakim ancestor, Joshua 14:15) or 'city of four.' The use of former names is a recurring narrative device in the conquest accounts, marking the historical transformation of cities and territories by their renaming.
11: From Hebron, Judah attacks Debir (dĕbîr), formerly qiryat-sēper — 'city of the scroll/book.' The name sēper (scroll, document) suggests a Bronze Age scribal or administrative center, consistent with ANE evidence for scribal cities. The transition from book-city to judge's conquest marks the reorientation of literacy and administration under the covenant people.
12: Caleb's offer: ʾašer-yakkeh ʾet-qiryat-sēper ûlĕkādāh wĕnātattî lô ʾet-ʿaksāh bittî lĕʾiššāh — 'whoever strikes and captures Kiriath-sepher, I will give him Achsah my daughter as wife.' The marriage-prize formula for military valor recurs in 1 Samuel 18:17 (Saul's offer to David). The narrative's interest is not Caleb's cultural convention but Achsah's subsequent agency in Judges 1:15.
13: Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother (ʾăḥî kālēb haqqāṭōn), captures the city. Qāṭōn (younger/smaller) echoes the pattern of the younger brother succeeding over older expectations (Jacob, Joseph, David). Othniel's Kenizzite descent makes him a non-Israelite ethnic incorporated into the covenant community — the same outsider-integration as Caleb himself (Joshua 14:6).
14: wattĕsîtēhû liš'ôl mēʾēt-ʾābîhā śādeh — 'she urged him to ask her father for a field.' The verb sûṯ (hiph, urge, incite) is used elsewhere for inciting to idolatry (Deuteronomy 13:7), but here positively for Achsah's initiative. wattispōaḥ mēʿal haḥămôr — 'she dismounted from her donkey' — the dismounting gesture formally signals a petition is coming, setting up the verbal request.
15: Achsah's request: hābāh-lî bĕrākāh kî ʾereṣ hannegeb nĕtattnî wĕnātattāh lî gullōt mayim — 'give me a blessing; since you have put me in the dry land, give me springs of water.' The word bĕrākāh means both blessing and gift/present — her request is itself a blessing-claim. The gullōt mayim (springs, literally bowls/basins of water) she requests are the ʿelyônôt and taḥtiyyônôt (upper and lower springs). Caleb gives both; the narrative rewards precise and bold petitioning.
16: The Kenites, descendants of Moses's father-in-law (ḥōtēn mōšeh), settle with Judah in the Negev of Arad. The ʾîr hattĕmārîm (city of palms) is the Jericho area. Ḥōtēn (father-in-law) marks the Mosaic family connection; the Kenite alliance (cf. Numbers 10:29-32) represents the ongoing incorporation of non-Israelite groups into the covenant community through loyalty relationships.
17: wayyaḥărîmû ʾōtāh — they devoted Zephath to ḥērem (destruction). The hiph'il of ḥāram marks sacred exclusion — the devoted city becomes YHWH's exclusively. wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šēm-hāʿîr ḥormāh — it was renamed Hormah from the same root (ḥerem), making the name a permanent lexical memorial of the devotion to destruction: the city's identity becomes its fate.
18: Judah captures Gaza, Ashkelon, and Ekron — three of the five Philistine city-states. The LXX reads 'did not capture' rather than 'captured,' and the conflict with Joshua 13:3 and Judges 3:3 (Philistine lords still active) supports the LXX reading. The coastal plain remained effectively Philistine territory throughout the judges period despite momentary successes.
19: wayhî YHWH ʾet-yĕhûdāh wayyōreš ʾet-hāhār kî lōʾ lĕhôrîš ʾet-yōšĕbê hāʿēmeq kî-rekeb barzel lāhem — 'YHWH was with Judah; he took the hill country, but could not drive out the valley inhabitants for they had iron chariots.' The lōʾ lĕhôrîš failure formula appears here first; it will recur through Judges 1:35. The rekeb barzel (iron chariots) represent Late Bronze/Iron I military technology giving tactical superiority in open terrain — the theological tension (YHWH with them, yet failure) is the honest reckoning of incomplete obedience meeting incomplete faith.
20: Caleb drives out the three sons of Anak from Hebron (wayyōreš miššām ʾet-šĕlōšâ bĕnê hāʿănāq). The Anakim had terrorized the wilderness spies (Numbers 13:33); Caleb's victory completes what Moses had specifically promised him (Joshua 14:12). The phrase kăʾašer dibber mōšeh (as Moses had said) marks this as a fulfilled-promise event, contrasting with the partial failures surrounding it.
21: wĕʾet-hayyĕbûsî yôšēb yĕrûšālaim lōʾ hôrîšû bĕnê binyāmîn — 'Benjamin did not drive out the Jebusites from Jerusalem.' This is the first tribal failure report. The Jebusite persistence explains why Jerusalem remained a neutral city between north and south until David's capture (2 Samuel 5:6-9). The lōʾ hôrîš (did not drive out) formula frames the failure as disobedience rather than military incapacity.
22: wayyaʿălû bêt-yôsēp gam-hēm bêt-ʾēl waYHWH ʿimmām — 'the house of Joseph went up, and YHWH was with them.' The divine-presence formula YHWH ʿimmām is the decisive variable: its presence here signals success before any battle description. Its implicit absence from the tribal failure reports explains those failures. Throughout the DtrH, YHWH ʿimmô/ʿimmām is the single most important predictor of outcome.
23: The house of Joseph sends scouts to Bethel, formerly called Lûz. The name change from Luz to Bethel (house of God) was made by Jacob after his vision there (Genesis 28:19). The narrative's use of both names (former and current) preserves the theological memory — the place of divine encounter is now a military objective.
24: The scouts offer the man ḥesed in exchange for information: wĕʿāśînû ʿimmĕkā ḥāsed — 'we will deal with you in covenant loyalty.' The word ḥesed (covenant fidelity, loyal love) here functions as a pledge of protection — the same covenant-loyalty exchange as with Rahab and the Jericho spies (Joshua 2:12-14). The Rahab parallel is deliberate: another city's opening provided by an insider who receives ḥesed.
25: The man showed them the entrance; they struck the city (wayyakkû ʾet-hāʿîr lĕpî-ḥāreb) but released (šillaḥ) the man and his family. The verb šillaḥ (send away, release, set free) — used for freeing prisoners and slaves — fulfills the ḥesed pledge completely. The man is not merely spared but released with freedom, exactly as promised.
26: The man went to the land of the Hittites and built a city he named Lûz — preserving the old name in new territory. The founding of a city in Hittite territory named Luz marks the geographic exile of Canaanite culture: driven from Canaan but persistent in the north. The verse records cultural survival-in-exile as historical observation, not condemnation.
27: The Manasseh failure list: wĕlōʾ-hôrîš mĕnaššeh ʾet-bêt-šĕʾān — 'Manasseh did not drive out Beth-shean.' The formula lōʾ hôrîš + city list structures the remainder of Judges 1. Beth-shean controlled the Jordan Valley pass; Taanach and Megiddo controlled the Jezreel Valley. These strategic chokepoints remaining in Canaanite hands fragmented Israel's territory into disconnected highlands.
28: wayhî kî-ḥāzaq yiśrāʾēl wayyāśem ʾet-hakkĕnaʿănî lāmas wĕhôrêš lōʾ hôrîšô — 'when Israel grew strong, they put the Canaanites to forced labor, but did not drive them out.' The verb māras (forced labor, corvée) reflects economic exploitation of those not expelled. Strength was used for commercial benefit rather than covenant obedience — tolerating the prohibited because it was profitable.
29: wĕʾeprayim lōʾ hôrîš ʾet-hakkĕnaʿănî hayyôšēb bĕgāzer — Ephraim did not drive out the Canaanites at Gezer. Gezer remained a contested city until the Solomonic period, when Pharaoh took it and gave it as dowry (1 Kings 9:16). The city's position at the coastal plain's edge meant Ephraim's territory had a permanent enclave it could not control.
30: Zebulun did not drive out the inhabitants of Kitron or Nahalol — wayyēšeb hakkĕnaʿănî bĕqirbô wayhî lāmas — 'the Canaanites lived in his midst and became forced laborers.' The shift from expulsion-language to coexistence-language marks the accommodation: pragmatic exploitation instead of covenant obedience. The repeated formula documents systematic tribal compromise at the policy level.
31: Asher's failure list is the longest — six cities including Acco (Mediterranean port), Sidon (major Phoenician city-state), and four others. The coastal territory of Asher was economically rich but militarily dominated by Phoenician cities. The length of the list signals Asher's deepest accommodation with coastal Canaanite culture of any tribe.
32: wayyēšeb hāʾašērî bĕqereb hakkĕnaʿănî — 'the Asherites lived among the Canaanites.' The preposition shifts from earlier: other tribes had Canaanites living in their midst; for Asher, Israel lives among the Canaanites. The balance of cultural power has inverted — Asher is the minority living within the Canaanite world, not the majority tolerating pockets of non-expulsion.
33: Naphtali lived among the Canaanites at Beth-shemesh (bêt-šemeš = house of the sun, a solar cult site) and Beth-anath (bêt-ʿănāt = house of Anat, the Canaanite war goddess). The names of the cities Naphtali failed to expel expose the religious stakes: coexistence with solar and war-goddess cult centers is the seedbed of the syncretism Judges will diagnose throughout the period.
34: wayyilḥăṣû hāʾĕmōrî ʾet-bĕnê dān hāhārāh — 'the Amorites pressed the people of Dan back into the hill country.' Dan is unique: the only tribe actively driven back rather than merely failing to drive out. The Amorites' offensive confinement of Dan to the hills explains the Danite migration to the far north in Judges 18 — their original allotment became militarily untenable.
35: The Amorites persisted in Mount Heres and nearby cities until wattikbad yad bêt-yôsēp — 'the hand of the house of Joseph prevailed.' The idiom kābad yād (heavy/strong hand) signals dominant power — the same root as kābōd (glory/weight). Even Joseph's success produces subjugation (lāmas) rather than expulsion — the strongest tribe still settles for economic exploitation instead of the covenant command.
36: ûgĕbûl hāʾĕmōrî mimmāʿăleh ʿaqrabbîm minhassela wāmāʿlāh — 'the border of the Amorites ran from the ascent of Akrabbim.' The Scorpion Pass (maʿăleh ʿaqrabbîm) marks the southern boundary of persistent Amorite territory (cf. Numbers 34:4; Joshua 15:3). The closing geographic note confirms that the conquest ended with defined Amorite enclaves intact — the map was not fully redrawn.
